title: Dog Foreign Object Under Eyelid: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do if your dog has a foreign object under their eyelid. Discover why this is a high-priority emergency and how a photo helps vets.
content_html:
What Is a Foreign Object in a Dog's Eye?

A foreign object (or foreign body) is any external material that becomes lodged under your dog's eyelid or on the surface of the eye. Common examples include grass seeds, thorns, dirt, or small shards of plastic. Because the eye is one of the most sensitive parts of the body, even a microscopic speck can cause intense pain and rapid damage to the ocular tissues.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, a foreign object stuck under the eyelid is a veterinary emergency. Your dog’s natural instinct will be to paw at the eye, which can lead to corneal ulcers, punctures, or even permanent blindness within hours. You should contact an emergency vet immediately to have the object safely removed under professional supervision.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If your dog is calm enough, taking a clear photo of the affected eye can be incredibly helpful for the triage team. A high-resolution image allows a veterinarian to see the position of the object and the degree of swelling or redness. This information helps the clinic prepare the necessary tools—such as local anesthetics or specialized flushing equipment—before you even walk through the door.

faqs.question: Can I try to remove the object at home?
faqs.answer: No. Attempting to remove an object yourself can cause the dog to flinch, potentially leading to a corneal puncture. Vets often use topical numbing drops or sedation to remove objects safely.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a foreign body in a dog's eye?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include constant squinting, excessive tearing, thick discharge, a red appearance to the white of the eye, and the dog repeatedly pawing at their face.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: If possible, place an Elizabethan collar (cone) on your dog to prevent them from pawing at the eye. Keep the car cool and try to keep your dog as calm as possible.
